data.title: FNDB: A Bet On Valuation Mean Reversion In 2026
data.description:
The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF (FNDB) is a bet on mean reversion in relative valuation multiples. Its broad diversification across 1,600+ stocks with a fundamental (rather than market cap) weighting approach could be valuable in 2026. Given overheated growth in the US stock market, FNDB’s sizable tilt to the value factor by overweighting cheaper, profitable companies could prove as its strength if high valuations come into focus and inflation remains elevated this year.

FNDB
Name Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market ETF
Management Style Passive
Expense ratio 0.25%
Dividend Yield 1.52%
Assets $1.22B
Average Market Cap $112.6B
Morningstar Rating Silver
Number of Holdings 1,654
Top Sector Technology (18%)
Top 10 Concentration 19%
Portfolio Turnover 11%

Source: Seeking Alpha and Morningstar

Why FNDB Is Different From Other Large Value ETFs

FNDB tracks the performance of the RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity US All Index. This index is based on contrarian investing that gives a fundamental value tilt. The upshot is this. If we take any popular index like the S&P 500 or the Nasdaq-100, they weight their holdings by the market capitalization. But, the economics research argues that the market cap is not necessarily a good measure for a fair value. This can lead to overweighting overvalued and underweighting undervalued companies.

There was a paper written on fundamental weighting by Arnott, Hsu, and Moore in 2005. They showed that fundamental weighting can lead to better performance in developed markets on an absolute and risk adjusted basis. FNDB uses three equally-weighted fundamental metrics to sort stocks:

  1. Adjusted Sales (5-year average multiplied by the average equity over average assets).
  2. Retained Operating Cash flow (5-year average of operating cash flow - dividends - buybacks).
  3. Dividends plus buybacks (5-year average).

After applying liquidity filters, over 1,600 stocks get selected and weighted by their fundamental size. There are several very important implications from this selection process.

Fundamentals Drive FNDB's Weighting

First, FNDB holds almost all major names including big tech stocks like Microsoft, Meta, Apple and Nvidia. However, the weight it gives these companies is highly dependent on their fundamentals. For instance, here are the top 10 holdings:

We see the usual large-cap stocks. But, Nvidia and Tesla are conspicuously missing from the top 10 list. They are buried way under with less than 0.5% allocations. As stock prices for these two companies skyrocketed, their market cap has grown much faster than their fundamentals. Hence, the fund underweights them. In other words, FNDB does not go after price. Rather, it keeps its allocations tied to tangible economic footprints like revenues, cash flows and shareholders’ returns.

FNDB’s Diverse Market Cap

The second implication from this is that FNDB looks at all US stocks as long as they meet its liquidity filter. For this reason, the fund holds way more mid- and small-cap equities, according to Morningstar.

Relative to its large value category, FNDB has material exposure to smaller stocks. But, it is still small. If more small value exposure desired, I wrote an article on AVUV ETF that does an excellent job for this purpose.

FNDB’s Material Value Exposure

But most importantly, the fund tilts heavily towards the value factor. However, its value exposure is very different from other funds like Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV) that I recently analyzed. VTV uses an extensive model based on 11 factors to separate growth from value equities. Factors like sales and earnings growth rates, P/B, P/S and other things come into play. Like many other large value funds, VTV excludes from its portfolio anything classified as a growth stock.

Conversely, FNDB does something different. It does not apply fundamental filters to exclude stocks. Instead, the fund takes a more agnostic view instead. For this reason, it holds way more stocks in its expansive portfolio than a typical large value fund.

This gives FNDB more exposure to a wide variety of stocks that may give it an edge under certain scenarios. The most important factor is when fundamentals take center stage and valuations multiples revert to their mean. The fund takes a counter-cyclical stance and wins when growth normalizes and its valuations compress. All of this takes time for a broad market to catch up to these developments.

Conversely, FNDB can benefit here because, by its nature, it underweights momentum darlings that have appreciated a lot. For instance, when concerns around growth surfaced at the end of 2025, the performance diverged between FNDB vs. the S&P 500 and even the Nasdaq-100.

But, the fund has one shortcoming. It considers the 5-year averages of companies’ fundamentals. While fundamentals improvement or deterioration is usually a slow-motion process, it can happen rather quickly. There is some risk for FNDB to get caught holding value traps whose financial performance nosedives in a matter of one or two years. That’s one of the tradeoffs that an FNDB’s investor must grapple with.

FNDB's Sector Allocations

By its design, FNDB will still gravitate towards companies that have the largest sales, cash flows and payouts. Currently, those would be large-cap stocks in the technology sector. But, its overall sector allocations would be different from its peers. For instance, here is how the fund compares to the large value category:

Most importantly, FNDB underweights financial stocks and overweights tech and communications sectors. The communications sector is where tech stocks like Meta and Alphabet reside. This provides FNDB with important exposure to growth that many other large value funds lack or don’t have by their design. In this sense, FNDB can have a leg-up should growth stocks rally again in 2026.

FNDB Historic Performance

On a historic basis, FNDB showed very strong performance in an absolute or risk-adjusted basis relative to the large value category.

YTD 1-Year 3-Year 5-Year 10-Year
FNDB 8.9% 36.5% 18.8% 12.7% 13.5%
Large Value Category 6.7% 29.0% 15.5% 10.3% 11.0%

Source: Morningstar

The performance edge is material and visible across all time periods. Not only that, FNDB showed strong volatility-adjusted numbers too. Interestingly, FNDB’s standard deviation was not too different from its peers even though the fund has more exposure to growth and small-cap stocks.

3 Years 5 Years 10 Years
Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation Sharpe Ratio Standard Deviation
FNDB 0.93 12.44 0.59 14.94 0.71 15.93
Large Value Category 0.75 12.36 0.47 14.48 0.59 15.43

Source: Morningstar

For instance, if we compare FNDB to VTV and Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV), we see more or less the same performance till about 2020.

But since 2020, the divergence became clear and FNDB's performance edge has been growing. A lot of it has to do with that agnostic view FNDB casts on the total stock market. It does not exclude names with growth profiles. Instead, it assigns them weights depending on what each equity deserves based on its fundamentals.

As a result, FNDB naturally has more exposure to big names in the technology sector that have appreciated a lot since 2020. That has been FNDB's hidden edge over its peers. In this sense, FNDB does provide value exposure with optionality for capturing price appreciation from growth equities. If growth continues doing well, FNDB could outperform its large value peers.

What To Expect From FNDB In 2026

But, the biggest question is: what is in store for FNDB in 2026? The way the Iran conflict plays out, inflation is coming back in the US. The CPI rate spiked to 3.3% in March. Christopher Waller, the Fed governor known for his dovish stance, shifted his rhetoric in favor of "wait and see" after the beginning of the Middle Eastern conflict.

The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains shut, the more damage and inflationary pressures become visible. The main risk is for rising oil prices to make their way into the broad economy via various inputs like fertilizers, transportation and more.

Under the rising inflation scenario, there is a high probability that value can do better than growth in 2026. Under stress, the market may continue zooming on fundamentals. Many market analysts forecast no interest rate cuts in 2026 and, an interest cut pause can put valuations of high flying equities under stress. These circumstances favor value exposure just like it did in 2022-2023 when inflation spiked and growth concerns mounted. FNDB outperformed the Nasdaq-100 and the S&P 500 back then.

Of course, if AI fears subside and growth roars back, FNDB will lag the overall market. But, it will do better than its large value peers. That will be thanks to its somewhat above-average exposure to tech and cyclical names. Its exposure to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Intel and Meta stands at ~14%. These names are largely absent in large value ETFs. Plus, some of its holdings like Microsoft trade at relatively lower multiples to their own history. This can benefit FNDB if growth rally comes back and multiples revert to their means.

Investors Takeaways

FNDB provides useful exposure to the value factor. But, the way it does so is different relative to other large value ETFs. Instead of filtering out stocks completely, FNDB takes a broader view by weighting its holdings using fundamentals. This allows it to spread its bets across a wide universe of stocks and get a little bit of exposure to growth.

Historically, FNDB did the best when investors cared about fundamentals and mean reversion occurred. Inflationary risks are growing in 2026. This may shift investors’ focus to fundamentals and value again. But, even if we hit the middle ground and inflation turns out to be transitory, FNDB will still do better than the majority of its large value peers thanks to its above-average exposure to growth.

data.title: Realty Income: 3 New Reasons This Is Still A Hard Pass
data.description:
I last discussed Realty Income (O) in March, 2025. The stock has underperformed the SP500 (SPY) slightly through that period.

The reasons I noted for why Realty Income should underperform the market are contained in my article.

  • Realty Income: Three Reasons This Is Not A Buy

What's worse, Realty Income has stagnated for the last 3, 5, and 10 year periods, beaten by the SP500 in every category except YTD.

In reviewing the company again, I couldn't help but be underwhelmed by the company's prospects and unenthused by its current valuation. Following up on my original article, I'd like to highlight another 3 reasons why Realty Income remains a sell for my personal wealth-building journey.

1. Growth Can't Match the SP500

The SP500 is expected to grow earnings this year by 22%, next year by 17%, and 2028 by 20%. See below, circled in red.

Even with a fall in SP500 valuations to Liberation Day lows of 21x earnings, that still represents a 12.1% CAGR for 2.5 years. Should valuations remain where they've been since the start of the COVID-19 episode, the CAGR for the SP500 would be 16.8% for 2.5 years.

Over at Realty Income, the story could not be more subdued. Analysts expect 3% growth for the next three years. At current valuations and adding in the dividend, that's amounts to an 8% CAGR for 2.5 years. In an optimistic scenario where valuations return to those seen in the ZIRP era (Zero Interest Rate Policy, roughly 2008-2019) of 18.5x (compared to the current 15x), the CAGR for Realty Income shareholders would be 15.7% for 2.5 years.

What this means is that the most optimistic valuation scenario for Reality Income is worse than a notably pessimistic valuation scenario for the SP500.

2. Margins Are Not Exemplary, and Somewhat False

Let's compare Realty Incomes AFFO margins with those of their competitors, and than that of the SP500. Below are 5 main REITs covered often on Seeking Alpha and known well.

Realty Income (O) Terreno Realty (TRNO) Prologis (PLD) Agree Realty (ADC) NNN REIT (NNN)
AFFO/Revenue, TTM 69% 47% 52% 72% 71%
Valuation 15 32.3 31.2 18.1 13
Total Growth to Dec 2028 9.3% 23.6% 28.3% 14.7% 8.2%

As you can see, no metric stands out as spectacular with regards to Realty Income. It's cheap, but it's not growing well. Its margins are higher than industrial REITs, but lower than closer competitors ADC and NNN. In short, there is no "seven moons aligning" required for us to produce significant alpha.

Where the real divergence in quality and value comes is in comparison, again, to the SP500, keeping in mind that this is the standard to beat. But before I cover the SP500, let's chat about those "AFFO" figures.

AFFO is considered the recurring, adjusted free cashflow of the company. It's a standard marker of "earnings" for REITs, because adding back in large depreciation costs helps us understand more closely what the company can actually earn.

The problem: it doesn't account for net purchases of properties; it only accounts for maintenance capex. If we're going to fully devalue the capex of "megacap" tech stocks like the Mag 7 and look only at their true free cashflows, then we should be fully devalue the capex of Realty Income as well. If we do that, we see that Realty income invests more capital into its business than it pulls out: subtracting net property purchases from levered FCF in the TTM gets you $-1.3B in losses in the TTM. In fact, Realty Income has always bought more property than it has gained in AFFO.

Q4 reported figures were difficult to find on Realty Income's 43-slide Q4 investor presentation. The presentation details "who they are", their stability by design, fantastic its moat is, how diversified its tenant base is by industry and geography, their scaling potential and their expanding TAMs. Finally, despite all this, on page 40 we find that stated AFFO grew 2.1% year-over-year. The company's net debt to adjusted EBITDA is 5.5x, meaning there isn't debt-fueled growth left unless Realty is willing to sacrifice its A- credit rating.

The company issued $2.38B of common stock in the TTM, and bonds totaling $1.35B in the TTM. Realty Income is effectively profitless, because as I mentioned, we standard accounting subtracts ALL capex to arrive at free cashflow. It simply looks profitable because 1) AFFO does not subtract all capex; 2) Realty Income fills in the gaps with bonds and share issuance (outside stimulus). So it can look like Realty Income is a profitable, high margin business - after all, AFFO margin is a whopping 69% - but under the hood, it's pretty rickety. Most REITs do operate this way, but it still needs repeating.

Now lets look at the GAAP profit margins of the SP500, which do largely discount the capex of the Mag 7.

While the SP500 margins are objectively lower than stated margins at Realty Income, this set of businesses has materially improved in quality over the long haul, from debt-ridden, uninspired juggernauts in the late 20th century, to high-margin, contracted revenue, high cash "apex spawners", as superinvestor Mohnish Pabrai would say. This includes companies like Alphabet (GOOGL), which as created notable side businesses for its immense and captive audience of billions of Android users, and Meta Platforms (META), with additions WhatsApp, Threads and smartglasses as budding advertising and platform-based potential revenue streams of the future. These are the companies as part of the SP500 competing against Realty Income for your capital.

Over the long term, the SP500's margins can be expected to rise as dominant high margin companies continue to take over; its revenue can be expected to be further client-contracted as opposed to cyclical; its concentration in quality is expected to grow as weaker businesses give way to the increasing market capitalizations of stronger businesses. These are structural advantages a landowner like Realty Income can not reproduce.

3. Realty Income's Tenants Aren't Even SP500 Quality

Perhaps Realty Income's claim to quality could reside in the quality of its tenants. See below.

I decided to get a hard figure out of these metrics.

I took the credit scores of these 20 companies. I assigned a number, from 1-20, where 20 represents an AAA credit rating, while 1 represents a C credit rating. I then multiplied this by the weight of these tenants in Realty Income's portfolio (for example, 3.3% for 7-Eleven). This gave me a weighted average credit rating for the top 20 tenants of Realty Income. See below.

Tenant Credit Rating Score (1-20) Rent % As % of Top ~36% Weighted Value
7-Eleven A / A3 16 3.30% 9.2% 1.5
Dollar General BBB / Baa2 14 3.20% 9.0% 1.3
Walgreens BBB- / Baa3 12 3.10% 8.7% 1.0
Family Dollar BBB- / Baa3 12 2.60% 7.3% 0.9
Life Time Fitness B / B2 7 2.10% 5.9% 0.4
EG Group B- / B3 5 2.00% 5.6% 0.3
(B&Q) Kingfisher BBB / Baa2 14 2.00% 5.6% 0.8
Wynn Resorts B+ / B1 8 1.90% 5.3% 0.4

Sainsbury's BBB- / Baa3 12 1.50% 4.2% 0.5
BJ's Wholesale Club BB+ / Ba1 11 1.50% 4.2% 0.5
Tesco BBB- / Baa3 12 1.40% 3.9% 0.5
Tractor Supply BBB / Baa1 14 1.40% 3.9% 0.5
CVS Pharmacy BBB / Baa2 14 1.10% 3.1% 0.4
MGM (Bellagio) BB- / Ba3 6 1.10% 3.1% 0.2
Home Depot A / A1 16 1.10% 3.1% 0.5

LA Fitness NR (Est. B-) 4 1.00% 2.8% 0.1
Wal-Mart / Sam's Club AA / Aa2 18 1.00% 2.8% 0.5
TOTALS AVG: BB+ / BBB- 11.55 35.70% 100.0% 11.7

As you can see, the companies Realty Income has chosen as their top-tier quality tenants to focus on are below investment-grade level of BBB+. 35.7% of Realty Income's rent comes from BB+/BBB- companies.

Then I did the exercise for the same top 35.7% (as close to it as possible) of market capitalization in the SP500, starting with the largest concentrated position (Nvidia (NVDA)). See below.

Company Credit Rating Score (1-20) Index Weight As % of Top ~36% Weighted Value
Nvidia AA- 17 7.6% 20.8% 3.5
Apple Inc. AA+ 19 6.7% 18.3% 3.5
Microsoft AAA 20 4.9% 13.5% 2.7
Alphabet (Google) AA+ 19 5.4% 14.8% 2.8
Amazon AA 18 3.6% 10.0% 1.8
Broadcom A- 15 2.6% 7.2% 1.1
Meta Platforms AA- 17 2.2% 6.1% 1.0
Tesla BBB 14 1.9% 5.1% 0.7
Berkshire Hathaway AA 18 1.6% 4.3% 0.8
TOTALS AVG: AA 17.8 36.48% 100.00% 17.9

As you can see, 36.48% of the SP500's constitution maintains a weighted average credit score of AA. Let me show you how far above Realty Income's BB+/BBB- score that really is.

Credit Score Company

AAA

 Microsoft (MSFT)

AA+

 Alphabet (GOOGL)
AA SP500
AA- Nvidia (NVDA)
A+ Adobe (ADBE)
A ServiceNow (NOW)
A- JPMorgan Chase (JPM)
BBB+ Enbridge (ENB)
BBB General Motors (GM)
BBB- Global Payments (GPN)
BB+ Realty Income (O)

Conclusion

I have laid out three reasons why Realty Income doesn't meet my standard for investing to beat the SP500.

  1. The company's growth is almost nonexistent compared to the SP500;
  2. The company's true margins, counting capital expenditures, is negative, versus a very positive and growing SP500 profit margin;
  3. The roughly top third of the tenants of the company have a credit rating eight levels below the equivalent of the SP500.

It's for these three reasons, and the reasons listed in my original article, that Realty Income remains uninvestable to me. Further, with the recent price run-up, I'm rating this company a Sell.

Realty Income likely meets my parameters for a Quintuple Vortex. Readers looking for more hard looks at high debt, slow growth, dilutive companies should consider my article Enbridge: "Quintuple Vortex" Exemplified, where this term is explained, and see for themselves through the example of Enbridge (ENB) whether Realty Income is at this level of risk.

data.title: MGM Resorts: China Momentum Sets Up A Clean Beat
data.description:
MGM Resorts (MGM) has had a decent start to the year, up about 8%, but the ride has been choppy for most. The company is set to report earnings next week, prompting a good time to revisit drivers in the quarter and see what's in store for the full year earnings revision path. I believe investors should expect softness in the Las Vegas Strip results, but strength in Regional and noticeably positive results in MGM China, which can lead to a top-line beat and a clean, mid-single-digit magnitude adjusted EBITDAR beat for the quarter. The stock should start to close the gap versus its 2023 highs and I see upside for the shares in the near-term.

Business Profile

Taking a step back, MGM has three core divisions: Las Vegas Strip, MGM China and Regional operations. The way the company makes money is primarily through gambling and entertainment revenues at its casinos globally. The China operations in Macau have been the most volatile over the past few years, but we are past the large growth rate swings and the forward outlook is beginning to look steadier.

With that being said, the most profitable division is the Las Vegas strip with an adjusted EBITDAR margin of nearly 34%. The China business carries an adjusted EBITDAR margin of 27% and regional casinos are slightly accretive at 29.5%. With that being said, with COVID now several years removed, we are starting to see more consistency in operations making the forward growth forecast easier to project.

Source: Investor Presentation

The company also operates BetMGM, their online betting operation that has been growing rapidly. BetMGM reports separately from the company.

The company also has several major projects in development. For example, they have the Bellagio Dubai, ARIA Dubai and MGM Grand Dubai. These are all scheduled to open in 2028. The company is also under construction with MGM Osaka in Japan, scheduled to open in 2030.

Financial Profile

Coming out of last quarter, the company struck an upbeat tone. They were positive that they would grow in the Las Vegas segment, driven by lower interest rates, no tax on tips and overtime, better tax refunds y/y and better throughput from airline traffic at the Las Vegas airport.

The company doesn't guide, which makes earnings calls and conference updates all the more important. They do provide some information for modeling purposes, like corporate expenses and capex, but nothing in the way of revenue growth rates or EBITDAR margin guidance.

In order to understand how the company is faring in its operations, we actually want to take a multi-pronged approach. There tends to not be great high-frequency data, which means even retail investors can drive an edge on this name.

For Las Vegas, we'll start with Strip GGR, which is a public report done by the state of Nevada that focuses on all of the gambling revenue the companies take in. It can be correlated back to MGM's Las Vegas revenue in order to figure out the potential growth rate for the upcoming quarter.

Below is the compiled Las Vegas Strip gross gaming revenue growth rate by month. Now, strip revenues in the last few months haven't been great. January saw a sequential worsening versus December, posting -11% versus December's -6%. It did stabilize in February at 0.86% positive. We are still waiting on the March release, which tends to come out towards the very end of the month, but this can be inconsistent so we may not have it in time for the MGM earnings report. With that being said, March is a tougher compare than February but still nominally easy as they lap -4% GGR growth from the prior year period.

Source: Author Compiled

Source: Author Compiled

It should be noted that because of the Super Bowl in 2024 being in Las Vegas, the February result last year was considerably negative, which made for the compare. Also, I think it's worth noting that on a y/y, trends in Q1 were running down 6% through the end of February versus Q4 at essentially flat, so there's been a noticeable deterioration. March would've needed a sizable snap back in order for the strip to have seen growth. This may have been challenging given the geopolitical stress on the consumer and higher gas prices in the quarter.

For Macau, we'll follow a similar process to what we did with Las Vegas revenue. Macau has been a bright spot to help offset the Strip's weakness. You can see the improving upward trajectory since the start of 2025, which bodes well for MGM.

Source: Author Compiled

Source: Author Compiled

In Macau, the results in January, February and March were stellar, up 24%, 14%, and 14.3%, respectively, and this should lead to a very positive result for MGM. This follows mid-teens growth in the December quarter, so there's been a slight acceleration driven by January's results. Now, it's worth noting that January was the easiest compare of the year and compares only accelerate. As we get into April/May/June results from Macau, things should slow in this region. It won't necessarily be visible in the April because the compare is just 1.7%, but May is a +5% compare and June is a whopping +19%. This will be the true test for MGM in Macau.

Regional is much more difficult to triangulate. MGM's Regional footprint consists of Borgata, National Harbor, Grand Detroit, Springfield, and the Beau Rivage Resort & Casino. These are spread across multiple states in the US. An easy way to get a proxy for it is just to look at retail sales in the US for the casino control group

The American Gaming Association shows a 3.9% YTD total GGR increase. LSD% revenue growth is stable. February showed a nice acceleration versus January, but similar to LV Strip data, we don't yet have March data to help further triangulate sales.

Source: American Gaming Association

The net result of these three regions is the following:

  • Las Vegas Strip - sequentially worse, magnitude notable
  • Macau - sequentially modestly strong; nominally very strong
  • Regional - stable +LSD%

Forward Estimates and Valuation

With this in mind, street is modeling $4.37 billion in revenue for Q1 and $0.52 in EPS. Las Vegas Strip is 40% of sales, Regional Operations are 34% of Sales and MGM China (Macau) is 21% of sales. The balance is 3% digital (BetMGM) and Other.

A combined growth figure based upon what the data shows against the segment mixes is approximately 2.6% pre-BetMGM. After factoring in the BetMGM result of +6% proportional to MGM, we arrive at close to 3% growth for total MGM. This means my revenue estimate is now $4.4 billion, a beat versus street.

Then, to think about profitability, we turn to EBITDAR margins. Given revenue movements, regional should show slight expansion y/y, MGM China should show notable expansion y/y and both of those will be partially offset by Strip EBITDAR margins being down. Last year Strip EBITDAR margin was 36.7%, Regional EBITDAR margin was 30.1%, and MGM China EBITDAR margin was 28.5%. I'm modeling 35.5% for Strip this Q1, Regional at 30.5%, and MGM China at 31%.

Thus, I am shaking out to total adj. EBITDAR for the quarter, after adding in small contributions from BetMGM and Other, less stock compensation and other punitive items, to $661 million in adjusted EBITDAR. Street estimates for adjusted EBITDAR are not widely available, but given seasonality of MGM, adjusted EBITDAR in Q1 tends to be lower than that of Q4 (Q4: $635 million), I presume street is modeling something like $630 million. This would be a considerably positive result, then, to have adjusted EBITDAR 5% ahead. This translates to an EPS figure close to $0.60 versus street at $0.52.

A comparative valuation makes the most sense here. It should be noted that within the gaming universe of public companies, MGM is towards the higher end of the quality spectrum given a strong asset base on the Las Vegas Strip and a global presence with a leading online gaming platform. More regionally-focused operators or weaker asset base companies tend to trade at a discount.

  • MGM - 23.9x forward P/E and 8.6x forward EV/EBITDA
  • Wynn Resorts (WYNN) - 22.7x forward P/E and 9x forward EV/EBITDA
  • Las Vegas Sands (LVS) -17.8x forward P/E and 9.3x forward EV/EBITDA
  • Caesar's (CZR) - No P/E due to lack of tangible EPS, but 8.2x forward EV/EBITDA

MGM tends to trade at a premium to the group on a P/E basis, but on an EBITDA basis, it's essentially right in line with peers. For a higher quality asset base with an earnings beat coming, there is clear scope for upside, here, and the valuation spreads should not be this tight.

MGM has been on a choppy uptrend since this time last year and currently sits above its 50 and 200-day moving averages with momentum clearly running high as dictated by the RSI. To start to get back to 2023/2024 stock price levels, the company is going to need a string of clean earnings beats for a consistency pattern that generates a positive forward earnings revision curve. I'm looking for a $50 price target, which represents 20% upside from the current level and this would retest the 2023 highs.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

Investing in MGM means investing in a highly cyclical asset that is subject to even the slightest changes in consumer confidence and discretionary spending. Depending on how spending trends are evolving, MGM's revenue can fluctuate meaningfully quarter to quarter. Corporate spending and major events like the Super Bowl, depending on where and when they are held can also have significant timing and size impacts on growth figures for any given quarter. Geopolitics are another risk as they directly influence consumer demand and appetite for gambling and entertainment.

Conclusion

Despite weakness in Las Vegas Strip revenue this quarter, there is enough stability in the company's operations portfolio in Regional growing low-single-digit and Macau seeing explosive growth that the company can still beat street estimates for both top and bottom line. This is encouraging especially as the company starts to lap easier compares in the Strip and can see margin recovery later in the year. The stock doesn't trade at a wide multiple to peers despite a higher quality asset base and a clear earnings beat on the way, so I expect the stock can start to distance itself positively.

data.title: Beyond Market Cap: A Nearly 19-Year Case For Dividend-Driven Emerging Markets
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Nearly nineteen years is a long time in emerging markets. It spans both commodity price supercycles and commodity price collapses, the rise of China, the so-called ‘taper tantrum,’1 a pandemic shock and recovery, and a new era defined by geopolitics and supply chain realignment.

Through all of it, the WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund (DEM) has been live, navigating environments that have, at times, rewarded growth, punished value, and then shifted to the opposite. Ultimately, longevity matters. It offers an observable record of how a rules-based, income-focused approach behaves across cycles. At its core, DEM is built on a simple but often underappreciated idea: in emerging markets, dividends can be a powerful signal of discipline and shareholder alignment. Starting from that premise, the strategy tilts toward companies that are not just attractively valued but also returning capital along the way, creating a distinct profile that looks meaningfully different from traditional benchmarks, such as the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

In Figure 1a shows:

  • DEM's live track record spans multiple EM cycles, demonstrating how a dividend-focused approach behaves across radically different macro regimes.
  • The strategy has historically delivered periods of relative outperformance, particularly when value, income and capital discipline are rewarded over growth-led market leadership.

Figure 1a: A Nearly 19-Year Record Across Cycles: Income Tilt, Emerging Markets, and Long-Term Differentiation

Figure 1b: Standardized Returns

Sources: Morningstar, FactSet and WisdomTree, specifically, data is from the PATH Fund Comparison Tool, accessed as of April 12, 2026, but showing returns for the period ended April 10, 2026 for Figure 1a and March 31, 2026 for 1b. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance.

Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

With DEM, Income Isn’t an Outcome - It’s a Design Choice

At the core of DEM, which is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Index, is a deliberate decision: prioritize companies returning cash to shareholders with higher overall dividend yields and weight them by the scale of those distributions.

Importantly, this is not a screen layered on top of a traditional market-cap framework, but rather the foundation of the strategy itself. In emerging markets, where capital allocation discipline can vary widely, dividends offer a tangible signal. They reflect, in our view, potential signals around profitability, governance and a willingness to share earnings rather than simply reinvest or retain them.

By weighting companies based on total cash dividends paid, DEM systematically tilts toward firms with both the capacity and the commitment to generate income. The result is not subtle. It is a structurally higher yield profile that persists across time, shaping both the income characteristics investors receive and the types of exposures they ultimately hold, often leading to a portfolio that looks meaningfully different from traditional benchmarks.

Figure 2 shows the ‘dividend yield difference’ between DEM and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index benchmark, quantified as of March 31, 2026. The picture may not always look like this, but we do note that this is driven by underlying methodology at its core.

Figure 2: Engineered for Income: A Structural Yield Advantage in Emerging Markets

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar and FactSet, with data as of March 31, 2026. Subject to change. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Understanding the Longer Run Trends in Performance Attribution

When reviewing the total return performance of DEM against that of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, we recognize that no strategy with almost 19 years of live history should be expected to outperform at all times. The underlying WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Index has held a consistent focus on relatively higher dividend yielding emerging market stocks. As market leadership shifted and changed, we know that these types of stocks also did not always outperform lower dividend yielding, more growth-oriented equities.

The Strengths of a High Dividend Focus in Emerging Markets

DEM's strength showed up most clearly when emerging markets behaved as a broad, internally driven asset class. In those periods, its design, such as stock selection in India, selective exposure within China, and meaningful participation in Brazil combined with strong stock selection in consumer and communication-oriented businesses. The result is a portfolio that captures domestic growth, benefits from higher-quality balance sheets, and compounds steadily over time.

The Weaker Moments of a High Dividend Focus in Emerging Markets

By contrast, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index benchmark tended to lead when performance narrowed and became dominated by the technology supply chain. Here, DEM's underweight to Taiwan and South Korea, effectively an underweight to semiconductors, became a headwind, amplified by a lower allocation to Information Technology overall. These are not accidental gaps but the byproduct of a different design philosophy: one that prioritizes diversification, income, and quality over concentration in a single sector or region.

The insight, in our opinion, is that both outcomes are logical. DEM is built to win over full cycles, particularly when leadership broadens, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index has exceled in more concentrated, tech-led environments. Understanding that trade-off is essential to setting the right expectations.

Conclusion: Did You Know About Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.?

I phrased the ‘conclusion heading’ as a question because as I was looking over the numbers, I saw something that I was surprised by. The reason for my surprise was not that anything was ‘wrong’ or ‘incorrect’, but rather I just hadn’t realized how much TSMC (TSM), the company, had run in terms of its performance, thereby increasing its market capitalization relative to the rest of emerging market equities.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is weighted by float-adjusted market capitalization. Simply put, larger market cap companies with greater amounts of shares that regularly trade should see larger exposures.

But, as of March 31, 2026, TSMC had a weight above 13% in the MSCI Emerging Markets Index benchmark. I know about TSMC and how important this company is to the most advanced semiconductors on the planet. It makes sense that this is one of the world’s largest companies. But wow, I was surprised to see a single weight above 13% in this benchmark. Figure 3 shows the top 10 holdings of DEM versus the MSCI Emerging Markets Index to emphasize this point. I will clearly state that none of this tells us, with certainty, which of these two strategies will outperform going forward. However, we do know that many investors already have a lot of so-called ‘AI exposure’ and if they are thinking about a strategy that tilts away from that in emerging markets, DEM may warrant consideration.

In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006.
